salem-witch-trials.teaser: From James VI's Scottish witch panic to Martha (Barrett) Sparks in a Boston prison — and how the Barrett line carries forward into the Powers branch.

blocks.text: Leading — ideas from Europe: In the late 1500s a new Christian theory spread through theology and church law — that a witch had made a deliberate pact with the devil and that witches did not act alone. Healing the sick could be praised as "white witchcraft," but it was still witchcraft. Wholesale persecution followed.

In 1590, while James VI was king of Scotland (he later became King James I of England), more than a hundred people were accused of plotting his death through witchcraft. A violent storm on his return voyage from Denmark was blamed on witches; so were charges of melting a wax effigy of the king. Many accused were tried and executed. In 1597 James VI published Daemonologie, his views on witchcraft. Between 1603 and 1625 Scotland held roughly twenty witchcraft trials involving nearly 450 suspects — about half were convicted and executed.

Leading — Massachusetts before the panic: Puritan settlers founded Salem in the 1620s and the Winthrop Fleet poured into Massachusetts Bay from 1630. Thomas Barrett and Margaret (Huntington) arrived about 1635 and later settled in Chelmsford, thirty miles west of Salem, in 1663. Their son Joseph Barrett married Martha Gould in 1672; Rebecca Barrett was born in Chelmsford in 1673. Decades earlier, in 1648, the colony had executed its first accused witch — Margaret Jones, a Charlestown midwife and herbalist hanged at Boston's Gallows Hill. Between 1648 and 1693 about eighty people across New England were accused; fifteen were executed.

Leading — 1691 government: On 7 October 1691, King William and Queen Mary II rechartered the Massachusetts Bay Colony as the Province of Massachusetts Bay. England sent Sir William Phips as the new royal governor. The charter tightened crown control, extended religious tolerance beyond Puritan Anglicanism, and required oaths to the king. Puritans feared their religion was under attack again — at the very moment witch accusations were about to explode.

Our family's direct tie: The panic did not begin in Salem village. It began in Chelmsford when Martha (Barrett) Sparks — granddaughter of Thomas Barrett and Margaret (Huntington), daughter of Thomas Barrett Jr. — was accused of witchcraft. On 28 October 1691 she was sent to a Boston prison. She was thirty-five, married to Henry Sparks, who was away fighting in King Philip's War when she was imprisoned. Martha was never brought to trial. The History of Chelmsford records that Boston jailer John Arnold held Martha for fifty-eight weeks — and also held the more celebrated Salem prisoners Sarah Good, Sarah Osbourn, and Rebecca Nurse.

Wider panic while leadership was absent: In early 1692 the Massachusetts witch trials unfolded during governmental disruption. The Province of Massachusetts Bay was just getting started, and Governor Phips — sent from England — had arrived and immediately left for the western frontier to handle Native unrest, leaving a vacuum of leadership in Boston.

What is "Witchcraft"? Dad's document defines what witchcraft meant in the 1600s English American colonies before the panic changed the rules. It was a capital crime, but difficult to prove. Formally accusing someone was often punishment enough, because prisoners had to pay all their own expenses in jail whether guilty or innocent. Cases went through the regular court system and evidence had to meet ordinary legal standards. If the accused was found innocent, he or she could sue the accuser for slander or defamation to recover jail costs. Many accusations were common curses not meant to be taken literally — like calling someone a bad name.

In the spring of 1692, while Governor Phips was still away, those rules changed — and convictions became far easier. Witchcraft remained a capital crime, but courts now allowed "spectral evidence": a witness could testify that the accused person's spirit or spectral shape appeared in a dream while the accused's physical body was somewhere else. A special Court of Oyer and Terminer ("to hear and to determine") handled witch trials, allowed spectral evidence, and assumed the accused was guilty until proven innocent. Accused witches were shackled, denied food, and pressured to confess through intimidation, lack of legal counsel, and even torture. The accused still had to pay all court costs, and if found guilty had to forfeit all their property.

blocks.caption: Accused witches were arrested and put in leg irons and shackles, were denied food and were even tortured to make them confess.

blocks.text: Family neighbors in the hysteria: On 8 January 1692, Ralph Farnham Jr. died in Andover just before the witch trial of Mary Tyler, wife of Hopestill Tyler — he had been expected as a witness because witchcraft was blamed for his illness. On 30 July 1692, Ralph Farnham III and his brother John were summoned as witnesses in the trial of Martha Carrier, accused after her family brought smallpox into Andover; she was hanged on 19 August 1692. The Farnham line intersects our tree through Andover and later marriages in the Powers branch.

Release: On 1 November 1692, Thomas Barrett Jr. petitioned Governor Phips and the Council for Martha's release (Massachusetts Archives, Vol. 135 No. 64). He wrote that his daughter had lain in Boston prison for twelve months and five days on suspicion of witchcraft, committed by Deputy Governor Thomas Danforth, with no evidence produced and no accuser remaining; that Henry Sparks was still a soldier in the king's service in the eastern parts; and that Thomas had been caring for Martha's two young children and a lame, elderly wife in poor health. He begged the governor to order her release.

On 6 December 1692, Thomas Barrett Jr. appeared before the court and guaranteed a bond for Martha Carrier's appearance at the next session. Two days later — on 8 December 1692 — Martha (Barrett) Sparks was set free after fifty-eight weeks in prison. For more on the wider Massachusetts witch trials, Dad's document points to the "Story of Jessie Maud Paff branch of Morris Rankin Batie's Family Tree."

Lagging — into the Powers branch: Rebecca Barrett, born 4 July 1673 in Chelmsford to Joseph Barrett and Martha (Gould), married Walter Powers III on 16 December 1696 in Littleton. Walter worked as an innkeeper on the northerly part of Littleton near Brown Hill. On 22 September 1710 their son Josiah Powers Sr. was born in a Garrison House on Neshoba Hill in Littleton — the seventh and last child of Walter and Rebecca. That Josiah line carries forward through the Edith Powers branch to Winifred Eloise Coss.
